summary: McKenzie experiments with Squidoo, a content platform created by Seth Godin, as a tool for organic SEO linkbuilding. He describes the platform as a hybrid between wikis and blogs that allows users to create 'lenses' on specific topics. While he finds the concept promising for SEO since Squidoo lenses rank well on Google, he criticizes the AJAX-heavy editing interface as frustratingly slow and limiting. He creates a lens on teaching dolch sight words, strategically seeding it with links to his own properties. He notes that the platform's most popular content is dominated by spammy make-money-online schemes.
key_points: Squidoo lenses were reaching Google's first page for less competitive keywords, making it a viable SEO linkbuilding tool
The platform's AJAX-heavy interface was a case study in why web apps couldn't yet replace desktop software, with frustrating editing limitations like 2500 character paragraph caps
McKenzie strategically created educational content as a vehicle for SEO-valuable backlinks to his own properties
Despite Seth Godin's vision, Squidoo's popularity metrics were dominated by spammy credit repair and make-money-online content
money_quotes: Squidoo is a wonderful technology for Seth Godin and, well, somewhat less wonderful for its users.
Its also a poster-child for Why AJAX Will Not Replace Your Desktop In 2007.
I felt like I was spending as much time struggling against the platform as I was creating content.
Whats the worst that could happen? I spend an hour producing materials that help teachers and don't get paid for it. That strikes me as an hour well-spent.
I have handily preseeded it with two links controlled by myself, which is the payload of the entire lens for me.
Most of the lenses on top of the popularity metrics are Repair Your Credit And Make Lots of $$$ Online With No Money Down with a very high shady factor.
key_insight: Content platforms designed for user-generated knowledge inevitably become SEO arbitrage tools, useful primarily as linkbuilding vehicles rather than for their stated educational purpose.
